pathways.label: Path A — First-offender conviction expungement
pathways.summary: A first offender may file a motion to expunge a misdemeanor or felony conviction in the court where the conviction happened, as long as the person has not been convicted of a crime of violence, has no pending criminal proceeding, and has paid or obtained waiver/reduction of all court-imposed or court-related financial obligations. Rhode Island defines a first offender as someone convicted of a felony or misdemeanor who has not previously been convicted of or placed on probation for a felony or misdemeanor and has no pending criminal proceeding. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-1.htm")…
ruleClauses: A first offender may file a motion to expunge a misdemeanor or felony conviction in the court where the conviction happened, as long as the person has not been convicted of a crime of violence, has no pending criminal proceeding, and has paid or obtained waiver/reduction of all court-imposed or court-related financial obligations. Rhode Island defines a first offender as someone convicted of a felony or misdemeanor who has not previously been convicted of or placed on probation for a felony or misdemeanor and has no pending criminal proceeding. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-1.htm") ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm")
Conviction expungement for a first offender
Misdemeanor, single first-offender conviction 5 years from completion of sentence
Felony, single first-offender conviction 10 years from completion of sentence
Wilma should calculate the waiting period from completion of sentence, not from arrest or conviction date. (
"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm")
Multiple misdemeanor expungement

Rhode Island has a separate pathway for a person with more than one but fewer than six misdemeanor convictions and no felony conviction. This route allows a motion to expunge any or all of those misdemeanors after 10 years from completion of the last sentence. The person must also have no felony or misdemeanor arrest or conviction in the 10 years before filing, no pending criminal proceedings, and good moral character. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm") ("https://law.justia.com/codes/rhode-island/title-12/chapter-12-1-3/section-12-1-3-3/")
Important exclusions for the multiple-misdemeanor path:
Excluded from Multiple-Misdemeanor Path Statute / Category
Domestic-violence offenses under Chapter 29 of Title 12 Domestic Violence Prevention Act
Chemical-test refusal 31-27-2.1

Wilma should not say all DUI or all domestic-violence cases are automatically impossible under every route. The specific statutory exclusion appears in the multiple misdemeanor subsection. For any DUI, refusal, or domestic-violence case, Wilma should escalate or require exact statutory review. (
Deferred sentence expungement

If a person received a deferred sentence in Rhode Island, they may file a motion for expungement after completion of the deferred sentence. Under 12-19-19, if the court finds after a hearing that the person complied with all terms of the deferral agreement, including payment of fines, fees, costs, assessments, and restitution, the person becomes immediately eligible for consideration for expungement. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm") ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-19/12-19-19.htm")
Was this a deferred sentence under a written deferral agreement, and has the court found that you completed all terms?
A deferred sentence is not the same thing as an automatic expungement. The person still needs the court process and hearing.
Acquitted, dismissed, no true bill, no information, or otherwise exonerated

For non-conviction outcomes, Rhode Island uses 12-1-12 and 12-1-12.1. Identification records such as fingerprints, photographs, physical measurements, and other arrest identification records must generally be destroyed within 60 days after acquittal, dismissal, no true bill, no information, or other exoneration, and court records are sealed consistent with 12-1-12.1. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1/12-1-12.htm")
For District Court Rule 48(a) dismissals:
On or after January 1, 2023 Court automatically seals not less than 10 and not more than 20 days after dismissal
Before January 1, 2023 Court clerk administratively seals at defendants request; order is sent electronically to BCI and carried out within 90 days of receipt

For other acquittals, dismissals, no true bill, no information, or exonerations, the person may file a motion to seal. Notice must be given to the Attorney General and the police department at least 10 days before hearing. (
"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1/12-1-12.1.htm")

If your Rhode Island case ended without a conviction, you may need sealing rather than conviction expungement. Some District Court dismissals are automatic, but older dismissals or other exonerated outcomes may still require a request or motion.
Filed complaints under 12-10-12

Rhode Island allows certain complaints to be filed. If no action is taken during the filing period, the complaint is automatically expunged and no criminal record results. For domestic-violence filed complaints, if the defendant is not charged with another domestic-violence crime during the three-year period after filing, or if such a charge is dismissed or results in acquittal, all records relating to the filed domestic-violence complaint are expunged without a separate Chapter 12-1.3 motion. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-10/12-10-12.htm")
Was the complaint placed on file, and if so, was it a domestic-violence filing or a regular filing?

pathways.label: Path F — Marijuana-possession expungement
pathways.summary: Rhode Island has a special marijuana expungement statute, 12-1.3-5. A person with a prior civil violation, misdemeanor conviction, or felony conviction for possession only of a marijuana offense that has since been decriminalized is entitled to automatic expungement, notwithstanding the general Chapter 12-1.3 rules. Eligible records were required to be expunged before July 1, 2024, and the Chief Justice may provide an expedited procedure. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-5.htm") Conviction expungement for a first offender A first offender may file a motion to expunge a misdemeanor or felony conviction in…
ruleClauses: Rhode Island has a special marijuana expungement statute, 12-1.3-5. A person with a prior civil violation, misdemeanor conviction, or felony conviction for possession only of a marijuana offense that has since been decriminalized is entitled to automatic expungement, notwithstanding the general Chapter 12-1.3 rules. Eligible records were required to be expunged before July 1, 2024, and the Chief Justice may provide an expedited procedure. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-5.htm")
Conviction expungement for a first offender

A first offender may file a motion to expunge a misdemeanor or felony conviction in the court where the conviction happened, as long as the person has not been convicted of a crime of violence, has no pending criminal proceeding, and has paid or obtained waiver/reduction of all court-imposed or court-related financial obligations. Rhode Island defines a first offender as someone convicted of a felony or misdemeanor who has not previously been convicted of or placed on probation for a felony or misdemeanor and has no pending criminal proceeding. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-1.htm") ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm")
Misdemeanor, single first-offender conviction 5 years from completion of sentence
Felony, single first-offender conviction 10 years from completion of sentence
Wilma should calculate the waiting period from completion of sentence, not from arrest or conviction date. (
"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm")
Multiple misdemeanor expungement

Rhode Island has a separate pathway for a person with more than one but fewer than six misdemeanor convictions and no felony conviction. This route allows a motion to expunge any or all of those misdemeanors after 10 years from completion of the last sentence. The person must also have no felony or misdemeanor arrest or conviction in the 10 years before filing, no pending criminal proceedings, and good moral character. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm") ("https://law.justia.com/codes/rhode-island/title-12/chapter-12-1-3/section-12-1-3-3/")

Wilma should not say all DUI or all domestic-violence cases are automatically impossible under every route. The specific statutory exclusion appears in the multiple misdemeanor subsection. For any DUI, refusal, or domestic-violence case, Wilma should escalate or require exact statutory review. (
Deferred sentence expungement

If a person received a deferred sentence in Rhode Island, they may file a motion for expungement after completion of the deferred sentence. Under 12-19-19, if the court finds after a hearing that the person complied with all terms of the deferral agreement, including payment of fines, fees, costs, assessments, and restitution, the person becomes immediately eligible for consideration for expungement. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm") ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-19/12-19-19.htm")
Was this a deferred sentence under a written deferral agreement, and has the court found that you completed all terms?
A deferred sentence is not the same thing as an automatic expungement. The person still needs the court process and hearing.
Acquitted, dismissed, no true bill, no information, or otherwise exonerated

For non-conviction outcomes, Rhode Island uses 12-1-12 and 12-1-12.1. Identification records such as fingerprints, photographs, physical measurements, and other arrest identification records must generally be destroyed within 60 days after acquittal, dismissal, no true bill, no information, or other exoneration, and court records are sealed consistent with 12-1-12.1. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1/12-1-12.htm")
For District Court Rule 48(a) dismissals:
On or after January 1, 2023 Court automatically seals not less than 10 and not more than 20 days after dismissal
Before January 1, 2023 Court clerk administratively seals at defendants request; order is sent electronically to BCI and carried out within 90 days of receipt

For other acquittals, dismissals, no true bill, no information, or exonerations, the person may file a motion to seal. Notice must be given to the Attorney General and the police department at least 10 days before hearing. (
"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1/12-1-12.1.htm")

If your Rhode Island case ended without a conviction, you may need sealing rather than conviction expungement. Some District Court dismissals are automatic, but older dismissals or other exonerated outcomes may still require a request or motion.
Filed complaints under 12-10-12

Rhode Island allows certain complaints to be filed. If no action is taken during the filing period, the complaint is automatically expunged and no criminal record results. For domestic-violence filed complaints, if the defendant is not charged with another domestic-violence crime during the three-year period after filing, or if such a charge is dismissed or results in acquittal, all records relating to the filed domestic-violence complaint are expunged without a separate Chapter 12-1.3 motion. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-10/12-10-12.htm")

Rhode Island defines expungement as the sealing and retention of conviction or probation records and the removal from active files of records and information relating to the conviction or probation. The statutes definition of

includes court records, state/local police records, Bureau of Criminal Identification records, probation records, fingerprints, photographs, physical measurements, and other identification records; it excludes certain Attorney General files not kept by BCI in the ordinary course of BCI business. Separate from marijuana, Rhode Island allows a person to file for expungement of an offense that was decriminalized after the date of conviction. The court holds a hearing and may require the petitioner to show that the prior conviction would qualify as a decriminalized offense under current Rhode Island law. If conditions of the original sentence are complete and required fines/fees/costs have been paid, the court orders expungement without cost to the petitioner. sourceSections.title: Core Rhode Island Rule
sourceSections.text:
Rhode Island defines expungement as the sealing and retention of conviction or probation records and the removal from active files of records and information relating to the conviction or probation. The statutes definition of
records
includes court records, state/local police records, Bureau of Criminal Identification records, probation records, fingerprints, photographs, physical measurements, and other identification records; it excludes certain Attorney General files not kept by BCI in the ordinary course of BCI business. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-1.htm")
Wilma should explain:

In Rhode Island, expungement usually means the record is sealed and removed from active public files. It does not always mean every government, licensing, law-enforcement, federal, or private-background-check copy disappears.

sourceSections.title: Main Rhode Island Pathways
sourceSections.text:
Path A - Conviction expungement for a first offender
A first offender may file a motion to expunge a misdemeanor or felony conviction in the court where the conviction happened, as long as the person has not been convicted of a crime of violence, has no pending criminal proceeding, and has paid or obtained waiver/reduction of all court-imposed or court-related financial obligations. Rhode Island defines a first offender as someone convicted of a felony or misdemeanor who has not previously been convicted of or placed on probation for a felony or misdemeanor and has no pending criminal proceeding. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-1.htm") ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm")
Waiting periods:

Conviction Type Waiting Period
Misdemeanor, single first-offender conviction 5 years from completion of sentence
Felony, single first-offender conviction 10 years from completion of sentence
Wilma should calculate the waiting period from completion of sentence, not from arrest or conviction date. (

"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm")

Path B - Multiple misdemeanor expungement
Rhode Island has a separate pathway for a person with more than one but fewer than six misdemeanor convictions and no felony conviction. This route allows a motion to expunge any or all of those misdemeanors after 10 years from completion of the last sentence. The person must also have no felony or misdemeanor arrest or conviction in the 10 years before filing, no pending criminal proceedings, and good moral character. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm") ("https://law.justia.com/codes/rhode-island/title-12/chapter-12-1-3/section-12-1-3-3/")
Important exclusions for the multiple-misdemeanor path:

Excluded from Multiple-Misdemeanor Path Statute / Category
Domestic-violence offenses under Chapter 29 of Title 12 Domestic Violence Prevention Act
DUI 31-27-2
Chemical-test refusal 31-27-2.1
Wilma should not say all DUI or all domestic-violence cases are automatically impossible under every route. The specific statutory exclusion appears in the multiple misdemeanor subsection. For any DUI, refusal, or domestic-violence case, Wilma should escalate or require exact statutory review. (

"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm")

Path C - Deferred sentence expungement
If a person received a deferred sentence in Rhode Island, they may file a motion for expungement after completion of the deferred sentence. Under 12-19-19, if the court finds after a hearing that the person complied with all terms of the deferral agreement, including payment of fines, fees, costs, assessments, and restitution, the person becomes immediately eligible for consideration for expungement. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm") ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-19/12-19-19.htm")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a deferred sentence under a written deferral agreement, and has the court found that you completed all terms?

A deferred sentence is not the same thing as an automatic expungement. The person still needs the court process and hearing.

Path D - Acquitted, dismissed, no true bill, no information, or otherwise exonerated
For non-conviction outcomes, Rhode Island uses 12-1-12 and 12-1-12.1. Identification records such as fingerprints, photographs, physical measurements, and other arrest identification records must generally be destroyed within 60 days after acquittal, dismissal, no true bill, no information, or other exoneration, and court records are sealed consistent with 12-1-12.1. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1/12-1-12.htm")
For District Court Rule 48(a) dismissals:

Dismissal Date Rule
On or after January 1, 2023 Court automatically seals not less than 10 and not more than 20 days after dismissal
Before January 1, 2023 Court clerk administratively seals at defendants request; order is sent electronically to BCI and carried out within 90 days of receipt
For other acquittals, dismissals, no true bill, no information, or exonerations, the person may file a motion to seal. Notice must be given to the Attorney General and the police department at least 10 days before hearing. (

"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1/12-1-12.1.htm")
Wilma should say:

If your Rhode Island case ended without a conviction, you may need sealing rather than conviction expungement. Some District Court dismissals are automatic, but older dismissals or other exonerated outcomes may still require a request or motion.

Path E - Filed complaints under 12-10-12
Rhode Island allows certain complaints to be filed. If no action is taken during the filing period, the complaint is automatically expunged and no criminal record results. For domestic-violence filed complaints, if the defendant is not charged with another domestic-violence crime during the three-year period after filing, or if such a charge is dismissed or results in acquittal, all records relating to the filed domestic-violence complaint are expunged without a separate Chapter 12-1.3 motion. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-10/12-10-12.htm")
Wilma should ask:

Was the complaint placed on file, and if so, was it a domestic-violence filing or a regular filing?

Path F - Marijuana possession expungement
Rhode Island has a special marijuana expungement statute, 12-1.3-5. A person with a prior civil violation, misdemeanor conviction, or felony conviction for possession only of a marijuana offense that has since been decriminalized is entitled to automatic expungement, notwithstanding the general Chapter 12-1.3 rules. Eligible records were required to be expunged before July 1, 2024, and the Chief Justice may provide an expedited procedure. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-5.htm")
Important marijuana rules:

Marijuana Rule Wilma Logic
Possession-only offense Potentially eligible
Civil violation, misdemeanor, or felony Covered if possession-only and decriminalized
Amount not stated in record Court presumes amount was 2 oz. or less
Pending cases / prior convictions / violent convictions Do not block marijuana expungement under this statute
Outstanding fines/fees/costs Do not block; eligible marijuana-related amounts are waived
Other counts in same case Do not prevent expungement of the eligible marijuana count
A marijuana expungement under this section is not treated as a prior conviction or civil adjudication for sentencing a later crime or civil violation. (

"https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-5.htm")
Wilma should ask:

Was this marijuana possession only, or were there other charges like intent to deliver, distribution, weapons, violence, or non-marijuana counts?

Path G - Decriminalized offenses generally
Separate from marijuana, Rhode Island allows a person to file for expungement of an offense that was decriminalized after the date of conviction. The court holds a hearing and may require the petitioner to show that the prior conviction would qualify as a decriminalized offense under current Rhode Island law. If conditions of the original sentence are complete and required fines/fees/costs have been paid, the court orders expungement without cost to the petitioner. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-2.htm") ("https://law.justia.com/codes/rhode-island/title-12/chapter-12-1-3/section-12-1-3-3/")

Path H - Commercial sexual activity-related expungement
Rhode Island has a special expungement provision for certain commercial sexual activity offenses. Records of a person convicted, placed on probation, or whose case was filed under 12-10-12 for violations of 11-34.1-2 or 11-34.1-4 may be expunged one year after completion of sentence, and the court may grant the motion regardless of first-offender status. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE11/11-34.1/11-34.1-5.htm")
Wilma should treat these cases carefully because they may involve trafficking, exploitation, coercion, or victimization.

sourceSections.title: Court Standard for Expungement
sourceSections.text:
For conviction expungement, after a hearing, the court may grant expungement if it finds the statutory requirements are met. For a single misdemeanor or felony, the court looks at whether the petitioner has had no felony or misdemeanor arrests or convictions during the required lookback period, has no pending criminal proceedings, has satisfied or waived court financial obligations, has good moral character, has achieved rehabilitation to the courts satisfaction, and whether expungement is consistent with the public interest. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/rhode-island/title-12/chapter-12-1-3/section-12-1-3-3/")
For deferred sentences, the court must find compliance with all deferral terms, including payment of court-ordered fines, fees, costs, assessments, and restitution, no pending criminal proceedings, and good moral character. No person convicted of a crime of violence may have deferred-sentence records expunged. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/rhode-island/title-12/chapter-12-1-3/section-12-1-3-3/")

sourceSections.title: Filing Process
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Classify the case
Wilma should classify the Rhode Island matter as:

Conviction - single misdemeanor.
Conviction - single felony.
Multiple misdemeanor convictions.
Deferred sentence.
Acquittal / dismissal / no true bill / no information / otherwise exonerated.
Filed complaint under 12-10-12.
Domestic-violence filing.
Marijuana possession-only record.
Decriminalized offense.
Commercial sexual activity-related offense.
Federal, out-of-state, military, or tribal matter.
Step 2 - File in the court where the case occurred
A conviction expungement motion is filed in the court where the conviction took place. The Rhode Island Judiciary says the motion should be filed in the court where the conviction occurred, and the clerks office will fill in the hearing date. ("https://www.courts.ri.gov/programs-services/Pages/Judicial-Records-Expungement-Information.aspx")
Step 3 - Give notice
For conviction expungement, the filer must give notice of the hearing date to the Rhode Island Department of Attorney General and the police department that originally brought the charge at least 10 days before the hearing. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/rhode-island/title-12/chapter-12-1-3/section-12-1-3-3/")
Step 4 - Attend hearing
The court considers the statutory criteria, rehabilitation, public interest, financial obligations, pending cases, arrest/conviction lookback period, and any relevant information.
Step 5 - Deliver certified orders
If the motion is granted, the clerk prepares three certified copies of the order after all conditions are satisfied. One copy is for the petitioner, one is for the Attorney Generals BCI unit, and one is for the police department that charged the case. The Rhode Island Judiciary says the petitioner is responsible for delivering the copies to those agencies. ("https://www.courts.ri.gov/programs-services/Pages/Judicial-Records-Expungement-Information.aspx")

sourceSections.title: Effects of Expungement
sourceSections.text:
After expungement, the person is released from penalties and disabilities resulting from the conviction, except the expunged conviction may still be considered as a prior conviction if the person is later convicted of another crime. In most employment, license, civil-right, privilege, or witness contexts, the person may state that they have never been convicted of the expunged crime. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-4.htm")
Important disclosure exceptions:
Law enforcement agency employment.
Admission to the bar.
Teaching certificate.
Coaching certificate.
Early childhood education operator or employee.
Bar admission / character and fitness / disciplinary matters.
Certain law-enforcement charging or sentencing uses.
Court order. ("https://webserver.rilegislature.gov/Statutes/TITLE12/12-1.3/12-1.3-4.htm")
Wilma should say:

After Rhode Island expungement, you may usually say you were not convicted of that crime, but there are important exceptions for law enforcement jobs, bar admission, teaching, coaching, early childhood education, later sentencing, and some court or law-enforcement uses.

sourceSections.title: Effects of Sealing / Non-Conviction Relief
sourceSections.text:
For acquittals, dismissals, no true bills, no information, or other exonerated outcomes, Rhode Island law can require destruction of identification records and sealing of court and BCI records. The Judiciary explains that if the motion is granted, fingerprints, photographs, physical measurements, or other identification records taken by the Attorney General or law enforcement are destroyed, BCI records are sealed, and court records are sealed. ("https://www.courts.ri.gov/programs-services/Pages/Judicial-Records-Expungement-Information.aspx")
Wilma should say:

For a case that ended without conviction, Rhode Island may use sealing or destruction/sealing rather than conviction expungement.

sourceSections.title: Wilma Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand possible Rhode Island expungement or sealing pathways. This is legal information, not legal advice. Rhode Island has different rules for convictions, multiple misdemeanors, deferred sentences, dismissals, marijuana possession, decriminalized offenses, and filed complaints.

Core Questions
1 Was this case in Rhode Island?
2 Which court handled it?
District Court?
Superior Court?
Family Court?
3 What is the case number?
4 Do you know your BCI number?
5 What police department brought the charge?
6 Was the case:
Conviction?
Dismissal?
Acquittal?
No true bill?
No information?
Filed complaint?
Deferred sentence?
Marijuana possession-only?
Decriminalized offense?
7 Was the offense a misdemeanor or felony?
8 Was this your only conviction or probation?
9 Do you have multiple misdemeanor convictions?
10 Have you ever been convicted of a felony?
11 Was the offense a crime of violence?
12 Was it domestic violence under Chapter 29 of Title 12?
13 Was it DUI or chemical-test refusal?
14 Was there a deferred sentence agreement?
15 When was the sentence fully completed?
16 Have all fines, fees, costs, assessments, charges, and restitution been paid, waived, or reduced by court order?
17 Have you been arrested or convicted of any felony or misdemeanor during the required lookback period?
18 Are any criminal proceedings pending now?
19 Do you need this for employment, housing, licensing, teaching, coaching, childcare, law enforcement, immigration, or bar admission?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If out-of-state, federal, tribal, or military
Route:

Rhode Island state expungement or sealing likely cannot clear this directly. Use the court or jurisdiction where the case happened.

If dismissed / acquitted / no true bill / no information / otherwise exonerated
Route:

Check sealing/destruction under 12-1-12 and 12-1-12.1. If it was a District Court Rule 48(a) dismissal after January 1, 2023, it may be automatically sealed. Older cases may require a request or motion.

If filed complaint
Route:

Check 12-10-12. If no action was taken during the filing period, the complaint may be automatically expunged. For domestic-violence filings, apply the special three-year rule.

If marijuana possession-only
Route:

Check 12-1.3-5. Possession-only marijuana civil violations, misdemeanors, or felonies that were decriminalized are entitled to automatic expungement, with expedited procedure available.

If decriminalized offense
Route:

Check 12-1.3-2(g) and 12-1.3-3(e). Confirm the current law decriminalized the offense and that sentence conditions and financial obligations are complete.

If deferred sentence
Route:

Check 12-19-19 and 12-1.3-2(e). Confirm written deferral agreement, completion, hearing, no pending criminal proceedings, good moral character, and no crime-of-violence bar.

If single misdemeanor conviction
Route:

Check first-offender status, no crime of violence, 5 years after sentence completion, no arrests or convictions in preceding 5 years, no pending cases, good moral character, rehabilitation, public interest, and financial obligations paid/waived.

If single felony conviction
Route:

Check first-offender status, no crime of violence, 10 years after sentence completion, no arrests or convictions in preceding 10 years, no pending cases, good moral character, rehabilitation, public interest, and financial obligations paid/waived.

If multiple misdemeanors
Route:

Check more than one but fewer than six misdemeanors, no felony conviction, 10 years after completion of last sentence, no arrests or convictions in preceding 10 years, no pending cases, good moral character, and no excluded domestic-violence, DUI, or refusal convictions.

If crime of violence
Route:

Likely barred from conviction expungement. Attorney review required.

sourceSections.title: Mandatory Attorney Escalation Flags
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend legal help if any of these apply:
Felony conviction.
Any possible crime of violence.
Burglary, robbery, sexual assault, child molestation, kidnapping, manslaughter, arson, assault with intent, or larceny from the person.
Domestic violence.
DUI or chemical-test refusal.
Deferred sentence with unclear completion status.
Multiple misdemeanors close to the statutory limit.
Any felony conviction plus misdemeanor history.
Pending criminal case.
Arrest or conviction inside the required lookback period.
Unpaid restitution or unclear court financial obligations.
Immigration consequences.
Law enforcement, teaching, coaching, childcare, bar admission, or professional licensing issue.
Federal or out-of-state record.
Case includes both marijuana possession and non-marijuana counts.
User wants a guaranteed outcome.

12. Wilma
Do Not Say
Rules
Wilma should not say:

Your record will be erased from everywhere.

Nobody can ever see it again.

Rhode Island expungement deletes FBI records.

This fixes immigration.

This restores gun rights.

All dismissed cases need a motion.

All marijuana cases are automatically expunged.

All domestic-violence cases are impossible.

All DUI cases are impossible.

Multiple misdemeanors are always disqualifying.

Private background-check companies will update automatically.

Wilma may say:

Based on what you shared, you may have a Rhode Island expungement or sealing pathway. The next step is to verify the official court record, final disposition, offense type, sentence completion date, financial obligations, arrest/conviction history, and whether any special category applies.

Topic Rhode Island Rule
Main conviction statute R.I. Gen. Laws Chapter 12-1.3
Non-conviction sealing 12-1-12 and 12-1-12.1

Marijuana possession 12-1.3-5
Commercial sexual activity special relief 11-34.1-5

Rule 48(a) dismissal before Jan. 1, 2023 Clerk sealing at defendant request
